class FreqElement implements Comparable<FreqElement> {
    int val;
    int freq;
    int ts;

    public FreqElement(int val, int freq, int ts) {
        this.val = val;
        this.freq = freq;
        this.ts = ts;
    }

    @Override
    public int compareTo(FreqElement other) {
        if (this.freq != other.freq) {
            return Integer.compare(other.freq, this.freq); // higher freq comes first
        } else {
            return Integer.compare(other.ts, this.ts); // higher ts comes first
        }
    }
}

So, in the compareTo method:

- If this.freq < other.freq, return 1.

So, the compareTo method would be:

public int compareTo(FreqElement other) {
    if (this.freq > other.freq) {
        return -1;
    } else if (this.freq < other.freq) {
        return 1;
    } else {
        if (this.ts > other.ts) {
            return -1;
        } else if (this.ts < other.ts) {
            return 1;
        } else {
            return 0;
        }
    }
}

First, the helper class FreqElement:

class FreqElement implements Comparable<FreqElement> {
    int val;
    int freq;
    int ts;

    public FreqElement(int val, int freq, int ts) {

        this.freq = freq;
        this.ts = ts;
    }

        } else {

        }
    }

Then, in the FreqStack class:

import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;
import java.util.PriorityQueue;

class FreqStack {

    private Map<Integer, Integer> freqMap;
    private Map<Integer, Integer> lastOccurrence;
    private int counter;
    private PriorityQueue<FreqElement> pq;

    public FreqStack() {
        freqMap = new HashMap<>();
        lastOccurrence = new HashMap<>();
        counter = 0;
        pq = new PriorityQueue<>();
    }

    public void push(int val) {
        counter++;
        int currentFreq = freqMap.getOrDefault(val, 0) + 1;
        freqMap.put(val, currentFreq);
        lastOccurrence.put(val, counter);
        pq.add(new FreqElement(val, currentFreq, counter));
    }

    public int pop() {
        while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
            FreqElement elem = pq.poll();
            int currentFreq = freqMap.getOrDefault(elem.val, 0);
            int lastTs = lastOccurrence.getOrDefault(elem.val, 0);
            if (currentFreq == elem.freq && lastTs == elem.ts) {
                // Valid entry, process it
                freqMap.put(elem.val, currentFreq - 1);
                if (currentFreq - 1 == 0) {
                    freqMap.remove(elem.val);
                }
                lastOccurrence.remove(elem.val);
                return elem.val;
            }
            // else, continue to next element
        }
        // According to the problem statement, pop is called only when the stack is not empty, so this should never happen
        return -1;
    }
}

Wait, but in the pop method, after processing, we remove the element from lastOccurrence. But what if the element is pushed again? The lastOccurrence would be updated again in the next push.

Hmm, perhaps it's better not to remove it from lastOccurrence, because the next push will update it again. So, in the pop method, after processing, we should just decrement the frequency, and not touch the lastOccurrence, because the last occurrence is still valid if the element is pushed again.

Wait, no. Because when we pop, the element is being removed from the stack. So, the last occurrence should still be the same as before the pop, but the frequency is decremented.

Wait, perhaps the lastOccurrence map should not be modified in the pop method. Because the last time the element was pushed is still the same, regardless of whether it's popped.

Wait, no. Because when you pop, you're removing the element from the stack, but the last occurrence refers to the last time it was pushed, which doesn't change. So, in the pop method, we shouldn't modify the lastOccurrence map.

So, in the pop method:

- After extracting a valid FreqElement, we decrement the frequency in freqMap.
- We do not modify the lastOccurrence map because the last time the element was pushed is still the same.

So, the corrected pop method would be:

public int pop() {
    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
        FreqElement elem = pq.poll();
        int currentFreq = freqMap.getOrDefault(elem.val, 0);
        int lastTs = lastOccurrence.get(elem.val);
        if (currentFreq == elem.freq && lastTs == elem.ts) {
            // Valid entry, process it
            freqMap.put(elem.val, currentFreq - 1);
            if (currentFreq - 1 == 0) {
                freqMap.remove(elem.val);
            }
            return elem.val;
        }
    }
    return -1;
}

Wait, but lastOccurrence is still needed for future pushes. So, in the pop method, we shouldn't remove it from lastOccurrence. So, the line lastOccurrence.remove(elem.val) should be removed.

So, the corrected code would not touch lastOccurrence in the pop method.

Another thing to consider: when an element is popped, its frequency is decremented, but its last occurrence remains the same because it was pushed before. So, the next time it's pushed again, the last occurrence will be updated.

This should be correct.

Testing this approach with the example:

Push 5: freqMap {5:1}, lastOccurrence {5:1}, pq has (5,1,1).

Push 7: freqMap {5:1,7:1}, lastOccurrence {5:1,7:2}, pq has (5,1,1), (7,1,2).

When we call pop(), the priority queue's top is the one with highest frequency (3) and latest timestamp (6), which is 5. So, it's processed, frequency becomes 2.

Next pop: the top would be the next highest frequency. Now, the max frequency is 2, and the elements with frequency 2 are 5 and 7. The one with the latest timestamp is 7 (timestamp 4). So, 7 is popped, frequency becomes 1.

Next pop: the max frequency is 2 (only 5 has it, since 7 is now 1). So, 5 is popped again, frequency becomes 1.

Next pop: the max frequency is 1, and the elements are 5,7,4. The one with the latest timestamp is 4 (timestamp5). So, 4 is popped.

Which matches the example.

So, this approach should work.

Now, considering the efficiency:

Each push operation involves adding to the priority queue, which is O(log n), where n is the number of elements in the queue. Each pop operation may involve multiple extractions from the queue, each O(log n), until a valid entry is found.

In the worst case, each pop could scan through all the stale entries, leading to O(k log n) per pop, where k is the number of stale entries. However, given the problem constraints, this should be manageable.

The space complexity is O(n), as each element can be in the priority queue multiple times.

So, the time complexity for each push is O(log n), and for each pop, it's O(k log n), where k is the number of stale entries processed.

But in practice, the number of stale entries per pop is limited, so the average case is acceptable.

Thus, the solution should be efficient enough for the problem constraints.
</think>

To solve this problem, we need to design a stack-like data structure that allows pushing elements onto the stack and popping the most frequent element. If there is a tie in frequency, the element closest to the top of the stack should be popped.

### Approach
The approach involves using a combination of a frequency map, a last occurrence map, and a priority queue (max-heap) to efficiently track and retrieve the most frequent element. Here's a detailed breakdown:

1. **Frequency Map**: This map keeps track of how many times each element has been pushed onto the stack.
2. **Last Occurrence Map**: This map records the last timestamp when each element was pushed, helping to determine the most recent occurrence in case of frequency ties.
3. **Priority Queue (Max-Heap)**: This queue is used to prioritize elements based on their frequency (higher first) and their last occurrence (more recent first). Each entry in the queue is a tuple containing the element, its frequency, and its last occurrence timestamp.

When pushing an element:
- Increment its frequency in the frequency map.
- Update its last occurrence timestamp.
- Add a new entry to the priority queue with the updated frequency and timestamp.

When popping an element:
- Extract the top element from the priority queue.
- Check if the extracted element's frequency and timestamp match the current state in the maps.
- If valid, decrement its frequency and return the element. If not, continue to the next element in the queue.

### Solution Code
```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;
import java.util.PriorityQueue;

class FreqElement implements Comparable<FreqElement> {
    int val;
    int freq;
    int ts;

    public FreqElement(int val, int freq, int ts) {
        this.val = val;
        this.freq = freq;
        this.ts = ts;
    }

    @Override
    public int compareTo(FreqElement other) {
        if (this.freq != other.freq) {
            return Integer.compare(other.freq, this.freq);
        } else {
            return Integer.compare(other.ts, this.ts);
        }
    }
}

class FreqStack {

    private Map<Integer, Integer> freqMap;
    private Map<Integer, Integer> lastOccurrence;
    private int counter;
    private PriorityQueue<FreqElement> pq;

    public FreqStack() {
        freqMap = new HashMap<>();
        lastOccurrence = new HashMap<>();
        counter = 0;
        pq = new PriorityQueue<>();
    }

    public void push(int val) {
        counter++;
        int currentFreq = freqMap.getOrDefault(val, 0) + 1;
        freqMap.put(val, currentFreq);
        lastOccurrence.put(val, counter);
        pq.add(new FreqElement(val, currentFreq, counter));
    }

    public int pop() {
        while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
            FreqElement elem = pq.poll();
            int currentFreq = freqMap.getOrDefault(elem.val, 0);
            int lastTs = lastOccurrence.get(elem.val);
            if (currentFreq == elem.freq && lastTs == elem.ts) {
                freqMap.put(elem.val, currentFreq - 1);
                if (currentFreq - 1 == 0) {
                    freqMap.remove(elem.val);
                }
                return elem.val;
            }
        }
        return -1; // As per problem statement, this line should never be reached
    }
}
```

### Explanation
- **Frequency Map**: Tracks how many times each element has been pushed.
- **Last Occurrence Map**: Records the most recent timestamp for each element.
- **Priority Queue**: Manages elements based on their frequency and last occurrence, ensuring efficient retrieval of the most frequent and recent element.

This approach ensures that each push operation is efficient, and each pop operation efficiently retrieves the correct element by leveraging the priority queue and maps to handle frequency and recency. The solution efficiently handles up to the maximum constraints provided.
